title: About
text: Hi! My name is Katie Freeman, and I’m a junior at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln double majoring in advertising and public relations and sports media communication with a minor in english. I’ve been on the dean’s list every semester in college. Currently, I am a Runner/Agency Assistant at Swanson Russell. I assist with administrative tasks and day-to-day office operations. Previously, I was the marketing and special events intern at the Brain Injury Association of Nebraska, where I created advertising campaigns on Canva and Adobe Creative Cloud, managed and shared on social media accounts including Facebook and Instagram, and helped with the planning and execution of their annual conference. I also worked closely with the marketing coordinator to create advertising strategies to help spread brain injury awareness. I am a go-getter, self-starter, leader of group projects and a team player. This is my sports blog for SPMC 350: Sport Data Analysis and Visualization.

title: Is Pryce Sandfort the Most Effective High Volume Three-Point Shooter Among Big Ten Players?
text:
Examining the Three-Point Efficiency of the Husker Forward Among Other Big Ten Players.
Players who take a high volume of shots from beyond the arch usually see their efficiency dip. As defenses focus in on them, attempts become more difficult and consistency becomes harder to maintain. In college basketball, especially in a competitive league like the Big Ten, players generally fall into one of two categories. High-volume shooters or highly efficient ones. But, Pryce Sandfort may just fall into both. This raises an important question: Is Pryce Sandfort the most effective high-volume three-point shooter in the conference?
In order to answer my question, I looked at the 2025-2026 Men’s Big Ten Player Stats by Sports Reference. I defined high-volume shooters as players attempting at least five three-pointers per game. I measured efficiency by a players 3-point percentage. A combination of makes, attempts and accuracy helped to separate players who can maintain precision while taking a large number of shots.
Before evaluating efficiency, it’s important to understand how much Sandfort is shooting. Volume can separate players and Sandfort is sitting in a good spot in the conference. Focusing on just Big Ten players who average at least 5 threes per game, we can see right where Sandfort is.


Code
library(tidyverse)

#| warning: false # Exclude any warnings from the chunk

bigten <- read_csv("data/bigten.csv")

nu <- bigten |>
  filter(Team == "Nebraska")

nu5 <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5, Team == "Nebraska")

shooters <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5)

ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=shooters, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3PA`), weight=`3PA`), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu5, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3PA`), weight=`3PA`), fill="#e41c38") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="Player", 
    y="3-Point Attempts", 
    title="No One Shoots More Than Sandfort",
    subtitle="Among players averaging at least 5 threes per game, he leads the Big Ten in three-point attempts", 
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) 









Sandfort doesn’t just take a lot of threes, he takes more than anyone else in the Big Ten. However, this often comes with a downside. The more a player shoots, the more difficult those shots become, leading to lower percentages.
So, the question becomes: Is Sandfort sacrificing efficiency for volume?
To answer this question, we need to look at how Sandfort compares to other high-volume shooters while comparing efficiency.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggtext)

bigten <- read_csv("data/bigten.csv")

shooters <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5)

nu5 <- bigten |>
  filter(`3PA` >= 5, Team == "Nebraska")

ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data=bigten,
    aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, size=`3PA`),
    color="grey",
    alpha = .3) +
  geom_point(
    data=nu5,
    aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, size=`3PA`),
    color="#e41c38"
  ) +
  scale_size(range = c(1,10), name="3-Point Attempts") +
    geom_text_repel(
      data=bigten,
      aes(x=`3P`, y=`3P%`, label=Player)
    ) +
labs(
    x="3-Point Field Goals", 
    y="3-Point Percentage", 
    title="High Volume Doesn't Mean Lower Efficiency for Sandfort", 
    subtitle="Most high-volume shooters see their accuracy drop, but Sandfort proves that doesn't have to happen.", 
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot")









While many high-volume shooters drift toward a lower efficiency, Sandfort hovers around 50%. His position on the chart, high volume and strong in accuracy, puts him in a strong position in the conference (rare category in the conference). —
This chart doesn’t just show how well he shoots, but how often, which few players can combine at this level. — This chart doesn’t just show how often he shoots, but how well he shoots, which few players are able to combine at this level.
It’s impressive to be efficient at a high volume, it’s even more impressive to rank among the best in both categories.


Code
bar1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=shooters, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P%`), weight=`3P%`), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu5, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P%`), weight=`3P%`), fill="#e41c38") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="",
    y="3-Point Percentage"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

bar2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=shooters, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P`), weight=`3P`), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(data=nu5, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P`), weight=`3P`), fill="#e41c38") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x="",
    y="3-Pointers Made"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() 

#| warning: false # Exclude any warnings from the chunk

library(patchwork)

bar2 + bar1 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Sandfort Shoots More and Shoots Better Than Most",
    subtitle = "He leads the Big Ten in three-point attempts and sits among the conference's most accurate shooters.",
    caption="Source: Sports Reference | By: Katie Freeman"
  )  & 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









Sandfort leads the conference in three-pointers made per game and doesn’t fall far behind in accuracy, ranking near the top of the Big Ten in three-point percentage.
This combination is what makes Sandfort a dangerous player.
You can’t afford to leave him open because of his efficiency. You also can’t shut him down, because his volume ensures he will continue to get opportunities. This dual threat forces opponents into difficult decisions with every possession, and Sandfort holds the advantage.
He isn’t just a high-volume shooter. He isn’t just an efficient one. He is both! That’s what makes him arguably the most effective high-volume three-point shooter in the Big Ten.
This brings me to the conclusion that the Pryce is truly right!
